method2.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
I can’t write the section with the required inline citations because no source documents were provided.

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants, and the available sources do not describe any present-day resident labor force, settlement-based commerce, or local industry on the atoll.[1] Its main historical economic resource was guano; the United States claimed the island in 1857 under the Guano Act of 1856, but the deposits are now exhausted.[1] In the 1930s, the island was colonized from Hawaii, placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior, and used as a stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[1] The island is now managed primarily for conservation as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and as part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1] Howland is included among the U.S. Pacific Remote Islands, whose surrounding exclusive economic zones are identified in regional Pacific mapping, but the cited sources provide little island-specific socioeconomic data for Howland itself.[5]

==Time zone==
Howland Island is in the UTC−12:00 time zone, identified in nautical time-zone notation as “Y” or “Yankee”.[2] It shares this time zone with Baker Island, another United States Minor Outlying Island.[1][2] Because Howland and Baker lie on the western side of the International Date Line, they are the last places on Earth where a given calendar date remains valid under the “Anywhere on Earth” convention.[3] The islands are uninhabited, so the last inhabited places to experience the end of a day are generally considered to be Niue and American Samoa in UTC−11:00, one hour ahead of Howland and Baker.[3][5] The time difference between Howland Island and Kiribati’s earliest time-zone areas can be 26 hours, making Kiribati among the first places to enter a new day while Howland is among the last.[1][5]

==History==
Howland Island shows evidence of early Polynesian presence, and American whaling ships sighted and charted the island by the early 19th century.[4] The first recorded sighting was in 1822 by George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island.[2] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher of the New Bedford whaleship Isabella named the island Howland.[4]

The United States claimed Howland Island in 1857, along with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2] Guano mining began in the 19th century, with competing American companies involved in exploiting the deposits on Howland.[4] The mining enterprises ended in 1878 after the guano deposits were depleted.[4] Prior to 1890, guano had been mined on the island by both American and British interests.[3]

In 1935, colonists from the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panala‘au Project, arrived from Hawaii to establish a permanent United States presence in the central Pacific.[4] A settlement called Itascatown, named after the U.S. Coast Guard Cutter Itasca, was established on Howland.[4] The island was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936 and was developed as a stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

On July 2, 1937, Amelia Earhart and her navigator, Fred Noonan, disappeared near Howland Island while attempting to reach it after departing Lae, Papua New Guinea, during Earhart’s around-the-world flight.[2] During World War II, Howland served as a lookout point for the U.S. military.[1] The island later became part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system and was designated part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument in 2009.[2][3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island is part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge System and provides nesting and foraging habitat for seabirds and other marine wildlife.[1] The refuge was established in 1974 and originally included the island, its waters, and submerged and emergent lands from the mean low tide line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] Howland has no permanent inhabitants, and access is limited primarily to scientific and management activities.[2][3]  

In 2009, Howland Island was included in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, which was created to protect the marine environment and associated historic and scientific resources around several remote U.S. Pacific islands.[3][4] As part of the monument designation, the refuge boundary around Howland was extended to 12 nautical miles, while monument waters out to 50 nautical miles are cooperatively managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and NOAA.[4] The island and surrounding reef habitat support migratory seabirds, shorebirds, and threatened or endangered sea turtles.[1][3]  

Refuge management emphasizes biological surveys, habitat monitoring, seabird conservation, marine habitat exploration, and scientific research.[5] Management plans for Howland and related Pacific refuges proposed no public recreational uses, reflecting the priority placed on conservation and research.[5] The island habitat has been affected by invasive species in the past, including Polynesian rats, which were introduced in the nineteenth century and later eradicated.[4]

==Earhart Light==
A small lighthouse was built on Howland Island in 1937, when the island’s unpaved landing strips were being improved for Amelia Earhart’s planned refueling stop during her round-the-world flight.[3]  
Earhart and navigator Fred Noonan were expected to reach Howland on July 2, 1937, after departing Lae, New Guinea, but although radio transmissions from Earhart were heard on the island, the aircraft never arrived.[3][4]  
The beacon later became associated with Earhart’s disappearance and with the extensive search that followed in the surrounding Pacific.[1][5]  
Japanese aircraft attacked Howland Island in December 1941, damaging the lighthouse and ending the island’s brief period of settlement during World War II.[4][5]  
The station has been inactive since 1942, and the surviving structure is now regarded as a day beacon rather than an operating lighthouse.[3][5]  
The damaged beacon was rebuilt in the early 1960s, and later visits to the uninhabited wildlife refuge included work to restore it and commemorate two colonists killed during the wartime attack.[2][4]

==Image gallery==
A map of Howland Island shows the small coral island in the central Pacific Ocean, southwest of Honolulu, Hawaii.[1]  

A NASA astronaut photograph taken during ISS Expedition 10 on December 3, 2004, shows Howland Island from space.[2][3][4]  

The astronaut image shows the island’s coral-fragment surface and the white breakers that mark its surrounding active fringing reef.[2][4]  

NASA acquired the image with a Kodak DCS760C digital camera and an 800 mm lens from an altitude of 192 nautical miles, or 356 km.[2][3][4]  

High-resolution astronaut photographs of reefs and islands such as Howland Island are used to update maps, assess reef ecosystem health, and calculate bathymetry of the surrounding seafloor.[2][4]  

The images also illustrate Howland Island’s protected status as part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system and its role as nesting and foraging habitat for birds and marine wildlife.[2][4][5]
